Base cooling duct design by User: Layer35 on MakerWorld.com  ·  makerworld.com/en/models/995268-a1-enclosure-fan-mounting-with-filter
01 Project Overview & Design Attribution

This report documents the implementation and engineering rationale behind a two-stage enclosure management system designed for a Bambu Lab A1 FDM 3D printer operating inside an VEVOR collapsible enclosure. Long-duration print jobs in enclosed environments present two distinct engineering challenges: (1) elevated mainboard temperatures that can cause thermal throttling or premature component wear during extended runs, and (2) accumulation of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and ultrafine particles from the molten thermoplastic process, posing both equipment and operator air-quality concerns [1][2]. The main goal of this project being to implement an online avaliable cooling system, and design a HEPA + Activated Carbon filter housing to help mitigate printing fumes for apartment or close living quarter enviroments. Then apply engineering principles learned in relevant course material to analyaze the effects of these implimentations.

The system addresses both challenges through two independent but pressure-coupled sub-systems: a filtered side-inlet cooling circuit (adapted from an existing open-source design) and a custom dual-stage HEPA/activated-carbon exhaust filtration unit.

Design Attribution

The side-mounted fan duct assemblies and integrated filter housings are directly used from the open-source design published by User: Layer35 on MakerWorld.com (Model #995268 — A1 Enclosure Fan Mounting with Filter, link). My contribution consists of: (a) physical hardware implementation and integration, (b) minor modifications to fit the specific enclosure hardware, and (c) the complete independent design of the VOC exhaust filtration unit described in Section 4. Credit for the cooling duct geometry belongs to Layer35.

02 Engineering Principles & Risk Mitigation

2.1  Thermal Risk Identification

Enclosed FDM printers accumulate heat from stepper motors, the hotend heater block, and the heated build plate. The printer mainboard, located beneath the gantry, operates within a manufacturer-specified temperature range. Sustained ambient temperatures above this threshold — common during multi-hour enclosed prints — can degrade component reliability over time [3]. Forced convection cooling via dedicated inlet fans is a well-established mitigation approach in electronics enclosure design [3][4].

Risk Likelihood Consequence Mitigation Applied
Mainboard overheating (long prints) Medium Thermal throttling / component wear Forced convection via filtered side-inlet fans
Dust ingestion into fans / PCB High (open fan without filter) Fan bearing wear, potential PCB shorts Filter media insert on each inlet fan
VOC / particle accumulation Medium (enclosed space) Operator IAQ / health risk Dual-stage HEPA + activated carbon exhaust unit
Print quality disruption from airflow Low (if designed carefully) Layer delamination, warping Low-velocity laminar inlet; exhaust positioned away from print area
Table 1 — Risk Register: Identified Hazards and Engineering Mitigations

2.2  Pressure Differential Airflow Strategy

Rather than simple open-loop ventilation, the system uses deliberate pressure differential management — a principle used in laboratory fume hoods, cleanrooms, and electronics enclosure design [4]:

  • Exhaust-Dominant (Slight Negative Pressure): The large PC exhaust fan driving air through the HEPA/carbon filter creates a net outflow condition. Any leakage paths in the enclosure panels draw ambient room air inward, preventing unfiltered fume-laden enclosure air from escaping — consistent with negative-pressure containment strategies [4]. Additional modifications and adjustments may be made to the filter housing and filter type to maximize outlet pressure and prevent any backpressure into the enclosure.
  • Filtered Inlet (Make-Up Air): The two side-mounted 5V fans supply fresh filtered air at the mainboard level, cooling the electronics while acting as make-up air for the exhaust fan.
  • Natural Pressure Equalization: Inlet air introduced at the sides naturally migrates toward the lower-pressure exhaust zone, sweeping across the mainboard before exiting through the filter — maximizing both cooling dwell time and contaminant capture.
  • Print Quality Preservation: Fan speeds are kept low to maintain gentle flow that does not disturb the thermal boundary layer around the print bed or introduce cooling gradients that could cause layer adhesion issues.
03 Side-Inlet Cooling Duct Implementation  (Base Design: Layer35 / MakerWorld)

The side-inlet cooling assembly consists of two symmetrical fan-mounting ducts positioned on the left and right panels of the enclosure. Each duct houses a compact 5V brushless fan backed by a filter media insert. The design was implemented as-published by Layer35, with minor geometric adjustments for the specific enclosure panel dimensions.

Fig. 1 — Left Cooling Duct with 5V Fan & Filter Slot
Fig. 2 — Right Cooling Duct (Mirror Assembly)

3.1  Filter Media — Dust Ingestion Prevention

Each inlet fan is equipped with a simple filter insert in the duct's dedicated filter slot. This addresses a frequently overlooked risk in enclosed printer installations: fan bearings and PCB traces are susceptible to conductive particulate contamination over time. Filtering inlet air extends service life for both the cooling fans and the mainboard [3].

Fig. 3 — Inlet Fan Filter Insert (Prevents Dust Ingestion into Electronics)

3.2  Implementation Notes

During installation, very minor dimensional adjustments were made to Layer35's original design to fit the specific panel thickness of the VEVOR enclosure. Cooling base was printed using PETG for improved thermal and impact resistance compared to PLA, given the moderately warm ambient temperatures expected inside a sealed enclosure during operation. However, the ducting pipes were printed out of TPU to maintain ductilty and the fan housing units were printed from PLA per recommendation from Layer35.

04 VOC & Particulate Exhaust Filtration System  (My Addition)

The VOC and fine-particulate filtration housing is my original design developed to address indoor air quality concerns for enclosed FDM printing in apartments or close living quarters. Unlike a passive enclosure that simply traps emissions, this simple system actively captures and neutralizes contaminants before they can accumulate.

4.1  Emissions Background — Why Filtration Matters

FDM printing produces two categories of airborne contaminants relevant to operator safety and IAQ [1][2][5]:

  • Ultrafine Particles (UFPs, <100 nm): Generated during filament melt and cooling. These particles are of respiratory concern due to their ability to penetrate deep lung tissue. PLA and PETG produce moderate UFP counts; ABS and ASA produce significantly higher concentrations [2][5].
  • Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs): Styrene (ABS/PETG), caprolactam (Nylon), and various aldehydes are released at printing temperatures. PETG emissions include ethylbenzene, toluene, and xylene at low concentrations; ABS emissions are considerably higher [1][5].
⚠ Scope Limitation: This filtration system is designed and informally validated for PLA, PETG, and TPU. For ABS and ASA — which produce higher styrene and VOC loads at elevated nozzle temperatures — a dedicated window-venting exhaust system is planned as a future design phase to provide direct atmospheric discharge.

4.2  Filter Unit Design — Dual-Stage Capture

Fig. 4 — Custom 3D-Printed Filter Housing & Large 5V PC Fan Mount
Fig. 5 — Dual-Stage Filter Media: HEPA (Stage 1) + Activated Carbon (Stage 2)
Figure 6 — SolidWorks CAD render of the filter housing.
Stage Filter Media Target Contaminant Capture Mechanism
Stage 1 HEPA (H13 equivalent) UFPs, larger particles, filament fragments Mechanical interception & diffusion capture at 0.3 µm MPPS [6]
Stage 2 Activated Carbon VOCs, styrene, aldehydes, odors Adsorption onto high-surface-area carbon matrix [6]
Table 2 — Dual-Stage Filtration Stages, Media, and Capture Mechanisms

4.3  Fan Sizing Rationale

The exhaust fan is a large-diameter 12V PC-style axial fan chosen to maximize volumetric flow rate at low static pressure — a critical constraint when forcing air through dense HEPA and carbon filter media [4][6]. Larger fan diameter produces higher flow at lower RPM, reducing acoustic output and extending bearing life. The fan is powered externally from a wall outlet which may be controlled with a simple on/off toggle switch.

4.4  Air Quality Validation (Qualitative)

System performance was evaluated using a portable air quality monitor measuring HCHO, TVOC, and CO2 levels during multi-hour PLA and PETG print sessions with the enclosure sealed and the filtration system running. Measured concentrations remained within acceptable indoor air quality ranges throughout all test sessions. Formal quantitative validation with calibrated instrumentation is identified as a future project milestone.

05 Air Quality Validation

To informally validate the filtration system's effectiveness, a portable air quality monitor was used during a PLA print session with the enclosure sealed and the filtration fan running. While this is not rigorous laboratory data acquisition, the results provide a practical, real-world indication that the dual-stage HEPA/activated-carbon filter is functioning as intended for everyday printing with low-emission filaments.

Figure 7 — Portable air quality monitor readings during an active PLA print session. CO₂, TVOC, and HCHO all within "Good" range with filtration system active.

Readings show CO₂ at 738 ppm, TVOC at 0.166 mg/m³, and formaldehyde (HCHO) at 0.029 mg/m³ — all registering in the "Good" band on the monitor. Temperature and humidity were recorded at 73 °F and 38% RH respectively. These readings suggest the system maintains acceptable indoor air quality for PLA, PETG, and TPU printing in a home or workshop environment, consistent with the project's intended scope.

05 Integrated System Operation & Future Development

6.1  Unified Airflow Model

When operating simultaneously, the two sub-systems interact to create a controlled internal environment. The side-inlet fans (Stage A) introduce fresh filtered air at the mainboard level. This air absorbs heat from the electronics and rises with the thermal plume. The exhaust fan (Stage B) draws this warm, contaminant-laden air through the HEPA/carbon filter and discharges it externally. The resulting slight negative enclosure pressure ensures that leakage paths act as additional inlets rather than uncontrolled emission sources — consistent with established containment engineering principles [4].

The pressure-coupling between the inlet and exhaust fans is intentional: In theory, the inlet fans prevent the exhaust fan from reaching a stall condition by maintaining sufficient supply air, while the exhaust fan prevents enclosure temperature rise that would otherwise reduce the effectiveness of the inlet cooling flow. While this principle may apply to industrial systems, a small scale system with small ambient air leaks will never reach a state where the exhaust fan can stall.

6.2  Filament Compatibility Matrix

FilamentSystem StatusNotes
PLA ✓ Fully Supported Low emissions; dual-stage filter provides ample margin [2]
PETG ✓ Fully Supported Moderate emissions (styrene, toluene at low levels); validated via air quality monitor [5]
TPU ✓ Fully Supported Low–moderate emissions; similar profile to PETG
ABS ⚠ Planned — Window Exhaust High styrene output; recirculating HEPA/carbon alone insufficient for extended use [1][6]
ASA ⚠ Planned — Window Exhaust Similar VOC profile to ABS; requires direct atmospheric venting
Table 3 — Filament Compatibility and System Support Status

6.3  Future Work — ABS/ASA Window-Vent System

The next planned design phase will replace the recirculating filter outlet with a direct-vent duct routed to a window opening, providing atmospheric discharge for high-VOC filaments like ABS and ASA. The side-inlet cooling circuit will remain unchanged in this configuration, ensuring mainboard thermal management is maintained regardless of the exhaust path.

06 Conclusions

This project successfully implements a coupled thermal management and air quality system for an enclosed Bambu Lab A1 3D printer. Takeaways:

  • Effective mainboard cooling through forced convection with filtered inlet air, mitigating thermal risk during extended print operations.
  • Theoretical pressure differential management strategy that prevents uncontrolled VOC emission through enclosure leakage paths.
  • A custom dual-stage HEPA/activated-carbon filtration unit providing informally validated (via portable air quality monitor) theoretically acceptable IAQ for PLA, PETG, and TPU printing.
  • Modular architecture supporting future integration of a direct window-venting exhaust for higher-emission filaments (ABS, ASA).
  • Practical application of fluid mechanic basics, thermodynamics, and engineering risk mitigation to a real-world additive manufacturing problem.
